cards.definition: a macronutrient providing energy — sugars (simple) and starch/fibre (complex). 4 kcal per gram
a macronutrient for growth and repair — made of amino acids. Sources: meat, fish, eggs, beans, nuts
a macronutrient providing energy and fat-soluble vitamins — saturated (animal) and unsaturated (plant)
solid at room temperature — associated with raised cholesterol and heart disease. Found in butter, cheese, red meat
liquid at room temperature — healthier than saturated fat. Includes monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats
indigestible plant material — aids digestive health, prevents constipation, may reduce cancer risk
amino acids the body cannot make — must be obtained from food. Complete proteins contain all 8
contains all essential amino acids — animal sources: meat, fish, eggs, dairy
lacks one or more essential amino acids — plant sources: beans, lentils, nuts
combining LBV proteins to provide all essential amino acids — e.g. beans on toast

questions.question: What is the function of carbohydrate?
options: A) Growth and repair of tissues
B) Providing energy — 4 kcal per gram
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates are the body's primary energy source — glucose from carbohydrates fuels cells, including the brain.

questions.question: What is the function of protein?
options: C) Growth and repair of body tissues — made of amino acids
questions.explanation: Protein is essential for growth, repair, and maintenance — it builds muscles, organs, enzymes, and hormones.

questions.question: What is the difference between saturated and unsaturated fat?
options: B) Saturated is solid at room temperature and linked to heart disease; unsaturated is liquid and healthier
C) Unsaturated is from animal sources
D) There is no nutritional difference
questions.explanation: Saturated fats raise LDL ('bad') cholesterol, increasing heart disease risk. Unsaturated fats are healthier — they can lower LDL cholesterol.

questions.question: What is dietary fibre?
options: C) Indigestible plant material — aids digestion and prevents constipation
questions.explanation: Dietary fibre is indigestible — it adds bulk to stools, aids digestion, and is associated with reduced risk of bowel cancer and heart disease.

questions.question: What are essential amino acids?
options: A) Amino acids the body makes itself
B) Amino acids from meat only
C) Amino acids the body cannot make — must come from food
D) Amino acids found only in complete proteins
questions.explanation: 8–9 essential amino acids cannot be synthesised by the body — they must come from diet. Complete proteins contain all essential amino acids.

questions.question: What is a high biological value (HBV) protein?
options: A) Protein containing no amino acids
C) Protein containing all essential amino acids — typically from animal sources
D) Protein with low nutritional value
questions.explanation: HBV proteins (meat, fish, eggs, dairy) contain all essential amino acids — they are 'complete' proteins.

questions.question: What is a low biological value (LBV) protein?
options: B) Protein lacking one or more essential amino acids — typically from plant sources
C) Protein with no amino acids
D) Protein containing excess amino acids
questions.explanation: LBV proteins (beans, lentils, nuts) lack one or more essential amino acids — they are 'incomplete' proteins. Combining LBV proteins provides all amino acids.

questions.question: What is complementary protein?
options: A) Protein found in dairy products
B) Combining LBV proteins to provide all essential amino acids
C) Adding vitamin supplements to protein
D) Cooking proteins to improve their quality
questions.explanation: E.g. beans on toast: neither beans nor bread contain all essential amino acids, but together they provide a complete amino acid profile.

questions.question: How many kilocalories does fat provide per gram?
questions.explanation: Fat provides 9 kcal per gram — more than double the energy of carbohydrate or protein (both 4 kcal/g).

questions.question: What is the function of unsaturated fat?
options: B) Provides energy, fat-soluble vitamins, and is better for heart health than saturated fat
C) Only found in animal products
questions.explanation: Unsaturated fats provide energy and fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K). They are associated with better heart health than saturated fats.

questions.question: Carbohydrates provide 9 kcal per gram.
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates provide 4 kcal per gram. Fat provides 9 kcal per gram.

questions.question: Saturated fat is solid at room temperature.
questions.explanation: Saturated fats (butter, lard, coconut oil) are solid at room temperature — unsaturated fats are liquid.

questions.question: Dietary fibre is fully digested and absorbed by the body.
questions.explanation: Dietary fibre is INDIGESTIBLE — it passes through the digestive system, adding bulk and aiding gut health.

questions.question: HBV proteins contain all essential amino acids.
questions.explanation: High biological value proteins (meat, fish, eggs, dairy) contain all 8-9 essential amino acids.

questions.question: LBV proteins can be combined to provide all essential amino acids.
questions.explanation: Complementary proteins: combining LBV sources (e.g. rice and beans) provides a complete amino acid profile.

questions.question: Protein provides 9 kcal per gram.
questions.explanation: Protein provides 4 kcal per gram — the same as carbohydrate.

questions.question: Unsaturated fats are associated with raised cholesterol and heart disease.
questions.explanation: SATURATED fats are associated with raised LDL cholesterol. Unsaturated fats are generally healthier for the heart.

questions.question: Essential amino acids can be made by the body.
questions.explanation: Essential amino acids CANNOT be made by the body — they must be obtained from food.

questions.question: Plant-based proteins are generally LBV proteins.
questions.explanation: Most plant proteins lack one or more essential amino acids — they are incomplete (LBV) proteins.

questions.question: Combining beans on toast provides complementary proteins.
questions.explanation: Beans and toast together supply all essential amino acids that neither provides alone.

questions.question: ________ are the body's primary energy source — providing 4 kcal per gram.
questions.explanation: Simple carbohydrates (sugars) provide quick energy; complex carbohydrates provide sustained energy.

questions.question: ________ is used for growth and repair — made of amino acids.
questions.explanation: HBV proteins contain all essential amino acids; LBV proteins lack one or more.

questions.question: ________ fat is solid at room temperature and linked to heart disease.
questions.explanation: Saturated fats raise LDL cholesterol — found in butter, cheese, and red meat.

questions.question: ________ fat is liquid at room temperature and better for heart health.
questions.explanation: Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats are healthier choices.

questions.question: ________ fibre aids digestion and prevents constipation.
questions.explanation: Fibre adds bulk to stools and is associated with reduced cancer and heart disease risk.

questions.question: ________ amino acids cannot be made by the body — they must come from food.
questions.explanation: There are 8-9 essential amino acids — all must be present for protein synthesis.

questions.question: ________ biological value proteins contain all essential amino acids.
questions.explanation: HBV proteins: meat, fish, eggs, dairy — 'complete' proteins.

questions.question: ________ biological value proteins lack one or more essential amino acids.
questions.explanation: LBV proteins: beans, lentils, nuts — 'incomplete' proteins.

questions.question: ________ proteins are LBV proteins combined to provide all essential amino acids.
questions.explanation: E.g. beans on toast — neither is complete alone, but together they are.

questions.question: Fat provides ________ kcal per gram — more than double carbohydrate or protein.
questions.explanation: Fat is the most energy-dense macronutrient.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: growth and repair — made of amino acids
solid, animal sources — linked to heart disease
indigestible — aids digestion and gut health
combining LBV proteins to provide all amino acids

cards.definition: a chemical reaction between amino acids and sugars when heated — causes browning and new flavours (e.g. toast, roasted meat)
the browning of sugars when heated — creates a sweet, nutty flavour
starch granules absorb water and swell when heated — thickens sauces and gravies
heat or acid permanently alters the structure of proteins — eggs coagulate, gluten develops
combining two immiscible liquids (oil and water) with an emulsifier — e.g. mayonnaise uses egg yolk (lecithin)
illness caused by eating contaminated food — common bacteria: Salmonella, E. coli, Campylobacter
spreading bacteria from one food to another — e.g. raw meat touching cooked food or salad
the temperature range 5°C–63°C where bacteria multiply rapidly
information required on packaged food — ingredients, allergens, use-by/best-before dates, nutritional info
substances causing allergic reactions — the 14 major allergens must be declared on food labels

questions.question: What is the Maillard reaction?
options: B) Chemical reaction between amino acids and sugars — causing browning and new flavours
D) Starch thickening when mixed with water
questions.explanation: Maillard reaction occurs above 140°C — produces the brown colour and complex flavours in toast, roasted meat, and baked goods.

questions.question: What is gelatinisation?
options: C) Starch granules absorbing water and swelling when heated — thickening sauces
questions.explanation: Gelatinisation: starch granules absorb water, swell, and burst when heated — thickening liquids. This is how sauces, gravies, and custards are thickened.

questions.question: What is the danger zone for bacterial growth?
questions.explanation: The danger zone (5°C–63°C) is where bacteria multiply rapidly. Food should be kept below 5°C (refrigerator) or above 63°C (cooking) to prevent growth.

questions.question: What is cross-contamination?
options: B) Spreading bacteria from one food to another
C) Cooking food at the wrong temperature
questions.explanation: Cross-contamination transfers bacteria — e.g. using the same chopping board for raw meat and salad vegetables. Always use separate boards/utensils for raw and ready-to-eat food.

questions.question: What is an emulsion?
options: B) A mixture of two immiscible liquids stabilised by an emulsifier
questions.explanation: Emulsions combine oil and water using an emulsifier (e.g. lecithin in egg yolk). Mayonnaise is an oil-in-water emulsion.

questions.question: What is caramelisation?
options: C) The browning of sugars when heated — creating sweet, nutty flavours
D) Bacteria growth on sweet foods
questions.explanation: Caramelisation occurs when sugars are heated above 160°C — they break down and create brown colour and complex flavours.

questions.question: What is denaturation of protein?
options: B) Protein permanently changing structure when heated or exposed to acid
questions.explanation: Denaturation permanently changes the protein's shape — e.g. egg white coagulating when fried, gluten developing when flour is kneaded.

questions.question: What are the 14 major allergens?
options: A) Any ingredient that could cause illness
B) 14 specific substances that must be declared on food labels — including nuts, dairy, gluten, eggs
questions.explanation: The 14 allergens (EU/UK law): celery, cereals containing gluten, crustaceans, eggs, fish, lupin, milk, molluscs, mustard, peanuts, sesame, soybeans, sulphur dioxide, tree nuts.

questions.question: What bacteria commonly cause food poisoning?
questions.explanation: Common food poisoning bacteria: Salmonella (raw poultry/eggs), Campylobacter (most common in UK — raw chicken), E. coli (undercooked meat, contaminated vegetables).

questions.question: What information must food labels include?
options: B) Ingredients, allergens, use-by/best-before dates, and nutritional information
D) Only the country of origin
questions.explanation: UK food labelling law requires: product name, ingredients list (with allergens highlighted), nutritional information, use-by or best-before date, storage instructions, and weight/volume.

questions.question: The Maillard reaction is the same as caramelisation.
questions.explanation: They are DIFFERENT: Maillard reaction involves amino acids + sugars → browning. Caramelisation involves sugar alone heating → browning.

questions.question: Gelatinisation thickens sauces when starch granules absorb water.
questions.explanation: Starch granules swell and burst when heated in liquid — thickening the sauce.

questions.question: The danger zone for bacterial growth is between 5°C and 63°C.
questions.explanation: Bacteria multiply rapidly in this temperature range — food should be kept outside it.

questions.question: Cross-contamination only occurs between raw meats.
questions.explanation: Cross-contamination can occur between any contaminated food and any other — raw to cooked or ready-to-eat foods.

questions.question: Denaturation is a reversible process.
questions.explanation: Denaturation is IRREVERSIBLE — the protein's structure is permanently changed.

questions.question: Allergens must be declared on food labels.
questions.explanation: UK law requires the 14 major allergens to be clearly indicated on food labels.

questions.question: Caramelisation occurs when sugars are heated.
questions.explanation: Sugars heated above approximately 160°C undergo caramelisation — browning and developing complex flavours.

questions.question: Emulsification combines two liquids that naturally mix.
questions.explanation: Emulsification combines two IMMISCIBLE liquids (that don't naturally mix) — oil and water — using an emulsifier.

questions.question: Salmonella is a common cause of food poisoning.
questions.explanation: Salmonella is found in raw poultry, eggs, and meat. Thorough cooking destroys it.

questions.question: Food kept at 70°C is within the danger zone.
questions.explanation: 70°C is ABOVE the danger zone (5°C–63°C). Above 63°C, bacteria are killed or their growth is significantly inhibited.

questions.question: The ________ reaction causes browning between amino acids and sugars when heated.
questions.explanation: The Maillard reaction produces the brown colour and complex flavours in toast, roasted meat, and pastry.

questions.question: ________ thickens sauces — starch granules absorb water and swell when heated.
questions.explanation: Gelatinisation is used to thicken gravies, custards, and soups.

questions.question: The danger zone for bacterial growth is ________ °C to 63°C.
questions.explanation: Bacteria multiply rapidly between 5°C and 63°C — keep food outside this range.

questions.question: ________ is spreading bacteria from one food to another.
questions.explanation: Use separate boards and utensils for raw and ready-to-eat food.

questions.question: ________ combines oil and water using an emulsifier — e.g. mayonnaise.
questions.explanation: Lecithin in egg yolk acts as the emulsifier in mayonnaise.

questions.question: ________ of sugars when heated above 160°C creates brown colour and complex flavours.
questions.explanation: Caramelisation is different from the Maillard reaction — it involves only sugar.

questions.question: ________ permanently changes protein structure — e.g. eggs coagulating.
questions.explanation: Denaturation is irreversible — caused by heat or acid.

questions.question: The ________ major allergens must be declared on food labels.
questions.explanation: Allergens include nuts, milk, eggs, gluten, fish, and shellfish.

questions.question: ________, E. coli, and Campylobacter are common food poisoning bacteria.
questions.explanation: Thorough cooking (core temp 75°C+) destroys these bacteria.

questions.question: Food labels must include ingredients, ________, nutritional info, and date marking.
questions.explanation: Allergens must be emphasised in the ingredients list — e.g. in bold.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: amino acids + sugars heated → browning and new flavours
bacteria spreading from one food to another
protein structure permanently changed by heat or acid
